text: FATSA, a town of Turkey in Asia, in the pash. Samsu, and 120 m. W of Trebisonde, on the Black sea, at the W extremity of a bay, and at the mouth of a small river of the same name. It contains an old palace, and an extensive khan, and still possesses a considerable trade with the Crimea, but is rapidly falling into decay. A little to the E are extensive ruins, supposed to be those of Polemonium. FATSA, FASA, or FESA, a town of Persia, in the FAU prov. of Fars, district of Darab, 80 m. SE of Shiraz, and 101 m. NNW of Lar, in a mountain pass. It is chiefly built of wood, and is said to be as large as Shiraz. Pop. 20,000. It has manufactories of silk, cotton, and woollen fabrics; and possesses a considerable trade in tobacco, the produce of the environs. This town is supposed by some to occupy the site of the ancient Pasargadac.

FATSISIO, or FATZIZIOU, an island of Japan, to the SSE of the prov. of Yzon, in the island of Nifon, on which it is dependent, in N lat. 33°, and E long. 140°. It is 20 m. in length, and about 7 in breadth, and contains a town of the same name. Its shores are steep and nearly inaccessible, but its soil is extremely fertile, and is well-cultivated. It is used as a place of banishment for state criminals, who are here employed in the manufacture of silk for the use of the court.
FATTICONDA, a town of Senegambia, in the kingdom of Bambuk, near the r. bank of the Fateme, 30 m. SW of Galam, and 420 m. ESE of St. Louis. It was formerly the capital of the kingdom.

FAUCIBUSSO, a town of Sudan, between Oulasso and Tumane. It is composed of huts, built of sun-dried bricks, with flat roofs. A large market is held here for colat-nuts, allspice, pepper, cloth manufactured in the country, salt from the Joliba, millet, cotton, pistachios, and other native produce. The cé and the nedé trees are very abundant in all this part of the country.—Caillie.

FAUCIGNY, or FAUSSIGNY, an administrative district and village of Sardinia, in the gov. of Savoy. The prov. is bounded on the N by the prov. of Chablais, on the NE by the Swiss cant. of Valais, on the SE by Piedmont, from which, and from the prov. of Tarentaise, Savoy Proper, and Genevois, on the SW, it is separated by the main ridge and by a ramification of the Pennine Alps, and on the NW by the prov. of Carouge, comprising an area 36 m. in length from NW to SE, and 21 m. in breadth. It is divided into 8 mandamento, and contains 69 communes. Pop. in 1821, 72,651; in 1839, 101,792. This prov. forms one of the most elevated districts in Europe, and comprises the entire basin of the Arve. In its eastern part, now commonly called Haut-Faucigny, are the lofty summits of Mont Blanc, Brevent, Aiguilles-Rouges, Buet, and others of inferior note, and the valley of Chamouni, the glaciers of which give rise to the Arve. The rearing of cattle forms the chief object of local industry. The village of F., 4 m. NW of Bonneville, is the cap. of the prov. It has an alt. of 2,162 ft. above sea-level. It contains the ruins of the castle, which gave its name to the barony from which the prov. has been formed. This barony was united to the domain of the counts of Savoy in 1233, and made part of the Swiss neutrality.

FAUCILLES, a range of mountains in France, extending W from the main body of the Vosges chain to the plateau of Langres, and separating the basins of the Meuse and Moselle from that of the Saone. On the NW it sends off two branches; one of which, named the mountains of Argonne, unites with the W. Ardennes, the other, sometimes called the mountains of the Moselle, joins that chain on the E.

FAUCOGNEY, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Haut-Saône, arrond. of of Sivas, and sanj. of Djanikili, 66 m. ESE of Lure. The cant. comprises 16 com. Pop. in 1831, 13,677; in 1841, 13,685. The town is 13 m. N of Lure, and 27 m. NE of Vesour, at the foot of the Vosges, on the Brenchin. Pop. 1,542. It has manufactories of linen and cotton fabrics, and extensive tanneries, and is noted for its kirschwasser. Fairs are held monthly. Iron and hone-stone are found in the environs.
FAUGERS, a village of France, in the dep. of the Herault, cant. of Bedarrieux, 17 m. N of Beziers, on the r. bank of the Albine. Pop. 644. Marble of various colours is found in the environs.
FAUGH AND FENTON, a township in the p. of Hayton, Cumberland, 71 m. ESE of Carlisle, on a branch of the Eden, and intersected by the Carlisle and Newcastle railway. Pop. 339.
FAUGHAN, a river in co. Londonderry, which rises in the Sperin mountains on the confines of co. Tyrone, runs 13 m. NW to within 11 m. of Londonderry, thence bends NE to the head of Lough Foyle.

FAUGHANVALE, a rivulet in co. Londonderry, which falls into the Atlantic.—Also a glen in the same co., in the upper part of the basin of the Faughan river.—Also a parish in the same co., 6 m. ENE of Londonderry, comprising an area 18,582 acres in extent, bounded on the W by the Faughan river, and on the E by Muffglen and Faughanvale rivulets. Pop. in 1831, 6,218; in 1841, 5,929: Pop. of village, in 1831, 123.
FAUGHART, a parish of co. Louth, 2½ m. NNE of Dundalk. Area 2,480 acres. Pop. 1,869.
FAUGHLEY, or FAUGHANSTOWN, a parish of co. Westmeath, 2 m. SW of Castle-Pollard. Area 7,050 acres. Pop. in 1841, 1,618. Its highest summit, Knockegone, has an alt. of 707 ft.
FAUGLIA, a village of Tuscany, in the emp. and 18 m. SE of Pisa. Pop. 400.
FAULD, a township in the p. of Hanbury, Staffordshire, 7½ m. ESE of Uttoxeter, on the S bank of the Dove. Area 860 acres. Pop. 56.

FAULHORN, a somewhat isolated mountain of Switzerland, in the cant. and 35 m. SE of Berne, and 3 m. from the SE bank of the lake of Brienz, between that lake and the valley of Grindelwald. It rises to the height of 8,140 ft. above sea-level, and has an hospice on its summit, whence an admirable view is obtained of the lakes, glaciers, and aiguilles of the Bernese Alps. Mr. Kämtz, the meteorologist, during 25 days' observations on the summit of the F. in the months of Sept. and Oct., 1831, observed the barometer to have a single minimum at 6 A. M., and a single maximum at 6 P. M. He likewise found the daily oscillations of the therm. to be smaller than on the plains during clear days; and the maximum of temp. to occur shortly after the culmination of the sun. The action of the direct solar rays was enormous; and he suffered several times severely from them, while the therm., in the shade, was under zero. Jupiter was often visible before sunset; and the polar star, and some others near the zenith, were generally visible 10 minutes after sunset. Neither the sun nor the moon, at rising or setting, exhibited the great apparent diam. which we observe in the plains. [Biblioth. Universelle.] The F. is formed of a species of black limestone rock, which easily dissolves into a mould of a deep black colour: whence perhaps the name.
FAULIRE, a mountain of France, in the dep. of Lozere, cant. of Chirac, 8 m. WNW of Marvejols.
FAULKBOURNE, a parish of Essex, 2 m. NW of Witham, and W of the Brain. Area 750 acres. Pop. in 1831, 161; in 1841, 157.

FAULQUEMONT, or FALKENBOURIEH, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Moselle, arrond. of Metz. The cant. comprises 31 com. Pop. in 1831, 16,927; in 1841, 16,927. The town is 21 m. E of Metz, on the r. bank of the Nied. Pop. 1,047. It has a manufactory of hosiery, and several tanneries.
FAULT-MAZURAS, a village of France, in the dep. of the Creuse, cant. and 1½ m. ESE of Boursheep. ganeuf. Pop. 430. Coal is wrought in the environs.
FAUMONT, a commune of France, in the dep. of Tras-os-Montes, comarca and 21 m. ESE of Villa- FAV the Nord, cant. of Orchies, 19 m. from Douai. Pop. 1,399.

FAUQUEMBERGUE, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Pas-de-Calais, arrond. of Saint-Omer. The cant. comprises 18 com. Pop. in 1831, 11,536; in 1841, 11,598. The town is 16 m. SSW of Saint-Omer, on the r. bank of the Aa. Pop. 1,979. FAQUEMONT, FALKENBERG, VALKENBERG, or Valkenburg, a commune and town of Belgium, in the prov. of Limburg, 8 m. E of Maestricht, on the Genli. Pop. 664. It is a place of considerable trade. Its fortifications were destroyed by the French in 1672, and in 1773 it was to a great extent destroyed by fire.
FAUQUEMONT (OLD), or OUD-VALKENBERG, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of Limburg, 10 m. E of Maestricht. Pop. 544.

FAUQUIER, a county in the state of Virginia, comprising an area of 520 sq. m., drained by the head-branches of the Rappahannock and Ocoquan rivers. The surface is pleasantly diversified, and the soil fertile. Pop. in 1840, 21,897. Its capital is Warrington, 102 m. N by W of Richmond.
FAURA, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 20 m. NNE of Valencia, partido and 4 m. N of Murviedo.
FAUROEULX, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of Hainaut. Pop. 449.

FAUSEMBAULT, a seignory of Lower Canada, partly in the p. of St. Augustin and partly in that of Saint Catherine, watered by the river Jacques Cartier, by lakes St. Joseph and Bonhomme, and by several small branches of the river Portneuf, which rise in the skirt of the mountains. The soil is generally fertile, and the timber good and abundant.

FAUSSE-RIVIERE, a lake of the state of Louisiana, U. S., formerly a portion of the bed of the Mississippi, and formed by a change which took place in the course of that river in 1714. Its banks are well cultivated, and extremely fertile. FAUSTINO (SAN), OF SAN FAUSTINO DE LOS Rios, a town of Venezuela, in the prov. and 70 m. SW of Merida, on the l. bank of the Rio San Faustina. It was founded in 1662. It has suffered much from the depredations of the Indians, and is now nearly reduced to ruins.

FAUSTINO, or RIO-DEL-ORO, a river of South America, which issues from a small lake, in the prov. and 20 m. SSW of Pamplona, runs N, passes San Faustino, and about 30 m. below that town unites with the Zulia. It derives its name from the gold dust which is found in its channel.
FAUTENBACH, a town of Baden, in the circle of the Middle-Rhine, SE of Bischofsheim. Pop. 847.

FAUVILLE, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Seine-Inferieure, arrond. of Yvetot. The cant. comprises 18 com. Pop. in 1831, 12,141; in 1841, 12,326. The town is 9 m. WNW of Yvetot, and 30 m. NW of Rouen. Pop. 1,490. Fairs are held 5 times a-year.
FAUVILLERS, or FAUXVILLERS, a commune and town of Belgium, in the prov. of Luxembourg, 9 m. E of Neufchateau. Pop. 1,131.
FAUX, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of Brabant, cant. of Court-Saint-Etienne. Pop. 108.— Also a commune in the prov. of Namur, cant. of Mozet. Pop. 580.
FAUX-FOSSES (LES), a hamlet of France, in the dep. of the Aube, cant. of Troyes. Pop. 1,002.
FAUX-LA-MONTAGNE, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Creuse, cant. and 32 m. S of Gentioux. Pop. 1,366. It has an extensive trade in
FAVAIOS, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Real, near the r. bank of the Tua. Pop. 1,400. It has several mineral springs.
FAVALE, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Basilicate, cant. and 5 m. W of Rotondella, near the r. bank of the Sinno. Pop. 1,100.

FAVARA, a town of Sicily, in the prov. and 4 m. ESE of Girgenti. Pop. 7,590. It has extensive sulphur mines.—Also a town in the prov. of Palermo, S of Termini.-Also a town of Spain, in Aragon, in the prov. of Zaragoza, partido of Caspe.
FAVARI, a town of Sicily, in the N confines of the prov. of Siragosa.
FAVARGE, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of Hainaut, cant. of Braine-le-Comte. Pop. 223.
FAVARQUES, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of Hainaut, cant. of Beloeil. Pop. 410.
FAVARTE-ET-BOURLIQUET, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of Hainaut, cant. of Frasnesles-Buissenal. Pop. 381.
FAVE, a river of France, in the dep. of the Vosges, which joins the Meurthe on the r. bank.

FAVERGES, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Isere, cant. of Tour-du-Pin. Pop. 1,246 .- Also a mandamento and town of Sardinia, in Savoy, in the prov. of Genevois, 16 m. SSE of Annecy, at the foot of a lofty hill, at the foot of which runs the torrent of Eau Morte. Pop. 3,641. It has an hospital, and possesses a cotton spinning-mill, cotton and paper mills, several tanneries, and considerable copper and iron works.

FAVERNEY, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of the Haute-Saone, cant. of Amance, 12 m. N of Vesoul, on the r. bank of the Lauterne. Pop. in 1841, 1,557. It has several tanneries, and carries on a considerable trade in wine and grain.
FAVEROLES, a commune of France, in the dep. of Cantal, eant. of Ruines, 8 m. S of Saint-Flour, on the summit of the rock of Mont-Chauson. Pop. 1,090. It produces good wine.

FAVERSHAM, a parish and seaport in Kent, a member of the Cinque-port of Dover. Area of p., 2,270 acres. Pop. in 1801, 3,488; in 1831, 4,429; in 1841, 4,621. The town stands about a <math>\frac{1}{2}</math> m. N of the London and Canterbury road, 9 m. W by N of Canterbury, on a navigable arm of the Swale, opposite the isle of Sheppey. It consists principally of four streets of considerable length, spacious and well-paved, in the centre of which stands the guildhall. On the opposite bank of the creek is Brent's town, consisting of a number of houses, most of them recently built, and chiefly inhabited by persons occupied at the port. The navigation of the creek has been greatly improved; and vessels of 100 tons burthen can now come up to the town at common tides; whilst, at spring tides, the channel is deep enough for ships drawing 8 ft. of water. F. has long been celebrated for its manufacture of gunpowder, under the superintendence of a branch of the ordnance established here. The various mills, storehouses, &c., are chiefly situated on a stream that rises in Ospringe p., and forms some small islands in its course to the Faversham creek. This manufacture is supposed to have been established here previous to the reign of Elizabeth. It was carried on by private individuals till 1760, when government, to insure greater security, erected buildings, and enless than 7 years after, a store, containing 25 barrels of powder, blew up, and, in 1781, a still more serious explosion took place, which shivered the works to atoms and nearly unroofed the town, with the neighbouring hamlets. The stores were now removed into the Marsh, a considerable distance below the town. During the late war upwards of 400 persons were employed in these works, which annually pro- 1,972. * FAW duced from 12,000 to 13,000 barrels. Government has for the last few years relinquished the powder works here, which are carried on by a private firm. The traffic of F. is very considerable in corn, hops, fruits, wool, &c., which are shipped here for the London market. Upwards of 40,000 quarters of corn have been annually shipped at this port. The oyster-fishery here is very considerable. F. oysters have been exported to Holland, to the yearly value of £4,000. Several fishing-smacks and a considerable number of coasting-vessels belong to this port. -The family of Duncombe take the title of baron from this town.

FAVIE'RES, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Meurthe, cant. and 6 m. SE of Colombey, 21 m. from Toul. Pop. 1,155. It has extensive manufactories of wood-ware and of pottery. Freestone is quarried in the environs.

FAVIGNANA, an island of the Mediterranean, in the group of the Ægades, 9 m. from the W coast of Sicily, in N lat. 37° 56' 36", and E long. 12° 17' 45" [Smyth]. It is 6 m. in length from E to W, and about 2 m. in average breadth. Pop. 4,000. Its surface is low, but is intersected from N to S by a range of hills which, towards the centre, forms a considerable summit, and is crowned by St. Catherine's castle. On the N coast is a deep bay, which affords good anchorage, and on which the chief town, San Giacomo, is situated. It has extensive tunny and anchovy fisheries, and possesses an active export trade in sheep, goats, poultry, and the produce of the fisheries. This island is the Egusa or Ethusa of the ancients, and was a considerable naval station of the Romans.
FAVORAN, or FOYRAN, a parish of co. Westmeath, 5 m. NNW of Castle-Pollard. Area 3,584 acres. Pop. in 1841, 1,774.
FAVORITE, a village of Baden, in the circle of the Middle Rhine, SE of Rastadt.

FAVOURABLE (Lake), or Deer's Lake, a small sheet of water in British America, in the district of Island. Two rivers issue from it, one of which forms the SW branch of the Severn; the other flows SW to Lake Winnepeg.

FAWCET-FOREST, a township, partly in the p. of Orton, and partly in that of Kendal, Westmoreland, 7 m. NNE of Kendal, and W of the Linn. It is of great extent, but its total pop. does not exceed 83 persons.
FAWDINGTON, a township in the p. of Cundall, N. R. of Yorkshire, 5\frac{1}{4} m. NNE of Borough-Bridge, and close on the N of England railway. Area 330 acres. Pop. 40.
FAWDON, a township in the p. of Gosforth, Northumberland, 3 m. NNW of Newcastle-upon- Tyne. Pop. 544.—Also a township in the p. of Ingram, Northumberland. Pop. 54.
FAWEUX, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of Liege, cant. of Olne. Pop. 175.—Also a commune in the same prov., in the cant. of Fleron. Pop. 79.
FAWKHAM, a parish in Kent, 5 m. SE of Dartford. Area 1,060 acres. Pop. 277.

FAWLER, a hamlet in the p. of Sparsholt, Berkshire. Pop. 144.—Also a hamlet in the p. of Charlbury, Oxfordshire, 43 m. NNE of Witnee, on the N gaged in the manufacture by its own agents. In bank of the Evenlode. Area 980 acres. Pop. 123.

FAWLEY, a parish of Buckinghamshire, 3 m. NNW of Henley-upon-Thames. Area 2,010 acres. Pop. 280.—Also a township in the p. of Dilwyn, Herefordshire. Pop. 142.—Also a township in the p. of Fownhope, in the same co. Pop. 57.—Also a parish in the co. and 6 m. SSE of Southampton, and W of Southampton Water. Area 6,590 acres. Pop.
FAWLEY (North), a parish in Berkshire, 5 m. S of Wantage. Area 2,870 acres. Pop. 225. It contains the liberty of South Fawley, with 44 inhabitants.
FAWN GROVE, a township of York co., in the state of Pennsylvania, U. S., 49 m. SE of Harrisburg, extending from Muddy creek to the confines of Maryland. Pop. in 1840, 859.
FAWN ISLAND, a small island of Upper Canada, in the river St. Clair, 18 m. below Port Sarnia. It comprises an area of about 50 acres.
FAWN RIVER, a township of St. Joseph co., in the state of Michigan, U.S. Pop. in 1840, 220.
FAWNS, a township in the p. of Kirkwhelpington, Northumberland, on the confines of Warwickshire. Pop. 8.
FAWSLEY, a parish in Northamptonshire, 41 m. S of Daventry. Area 1,550 acres. Pop. 48.

FAXARDO, a town of the island of Porto-Rico, 36 m. ESE of Santiago-de-Porto-Rico, on the E coast of the island, near the mouth of a river of the same name. Pop. 3,000. The surrounding district produces rice, maize, sugar, coffee, tobacco, and cotton in great abundance.

FAXARDO, an island of Venezuela, in the delta of the Orinoco, at the mouth of the Caroni. It is about 4 m. in length, and about 2 m. in breadth, and is divided by a channel into two parts. The W portion is low, and often submerged; on the other is a fortification for the defence of the navigation of the river from the Caribbee Indians.
FAXFLEET, a township in the p. of South- Cave, E. R. of Yorkshire, on the N bank of the Humber, at its confluence with the Trent. Area 1,630 acres. Pop. 358.
FAXOE, a village of Denmark, in the stift of Sieland. Limestone is quarried in the environs.
FAXTON, a parish in Northamptonshire, 5 m. SSW of Rothwell. Area 2,120 acres. Pop. 108.
FAY, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Loire-Inferieure, cant. of Blain, 9 m. NE of Savenay. Pop. in 1841, 3,712.
FAY (LE), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Saone-et-Loire, cant. of Beaurepaire. Pop. 1,504.
FAY-AUX-LOGES, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Loiret, cant. of Châteauneuf-sur- Loire, 12 m. ENE of Orleans. Pop. 1,271. It has extensive lime-quarries and kilns.

FAY-LE-FROID, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of the Haute-Loire, arrond. of Le Puy. The cant. comprises 6 com. Pop. in 1831, 7,028; in 1841, 7,823. The village is 20 m. E of Le Puy, near the r. bank of the Lignon. Pop. 879.

FAYAL, one of the Azore islands, in N lat. 38° 30', W long. 29°. It rises abruptly from the water; and is about 24 m. long from E to W, 9 m. broad, and 50 m. in circumf. It is fertile, and produces a large quantity of wine. Its good harbour, and its situation in the route of the homeward-bound ships from South America and India, has made it much frequented, as none of the neighbouring islands of Pico, Corvo, or Flores, affords secure anchorage for large vessels. In good years from 8,000 to 10,000 pipes of wine are exported from F., besides corn and provisions sufficient to freight 70 vessels of from 80 to 100 tons each. The pop. is about 24,000. There are several English, Scottish, and Irish settlers in Fayal.—The principal town, Villa Orta or Horta, is beautifully situated at the bottom of a semicircular bay, and at the foot of a grand amphitheatre of mountains, which are clothed to their summits with myrtles, laurels, and a variety of flowering shrubs. Pico, which is separated from F. only by a narrow channel, rises immediately in front of the town; but vessels are often driven from their moorings here FAY upon the craggy rocks of Pico or of Faval.-In the subjoined chart of the road of F., Point Espalamaca, the N part of the bay of F. is at a; the castle of Santa Cruz at b, and the castle of Greta at c; the port and town of Magdalena, on the island of Pico, are at d; and the Punta-del-Pie-de-Monte at e. The breadth of the channel betwixt the latter point and the extremity of the peninsular projection on which the castle of Greta is situated is 31 geog. m.
FAYCELLES, a commune and v. of France, in the dep. of Lot, cant. of Figeac. Pop. 1,045.
FAYE, a small town of France, in the dep. of Maine-et-Loire, 11 m. S of Angers. Pop. 1,297.

FAYENCE, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Var.—The cant., comprising 9 com., had a pop. of 10,833 in 1841.—The town, 12 m. NE of Draguignan, is noted for its manufactures of a species of brown earthenware, first made at Faenza in Italy, from which both the manufacture and this place derive their name. Pop. 2,544.
FAYET, a commune of France, in the dep. of Aveyron, cant. of Camares. Pop. 1,123.—Also a commune and v. in the dep. of Puy-de-Dome, cant. of Saint-Dier. Pop. 1,120.
FAYET-RONNAYES, a commune of France, in the dep. of Puy-de-Dome, cant. of Saint-Germainl'Herm. Pop. 1,143.

FAYETTE, a county of the state of Pennsylvania, U. S., comprising an area of 824 sq. m., bounded on the W by Monongahela river, and watered by Youghiogheny river, and Brown's, Dunlap, Redstone, and Cook's creeks. In the E are Laurel-Hill and Chesnut-Bridge. The soil is fertile and well-cultivated. Coal and iron are found in some parts, and it has also several salt and sulphur springs. Pop. in 1840, 33,574. Its capital is Union.—Also a county in the state of Virginia, watered by New and Gauly rivers, branches of Great Kanawha river, and the Clear and March forks of Coal river. Two m. from Fayetteville, the capital, is Long Point, a precipice which projects into New river, and rises to the height of 1,500 ft. above the level of the river. The summit forms a rocky plateau two acres in extent. Another insulated rock, named Hawk's Nest, or Marshall's Pillar, and several interesting remains of ancient fortifications, are also to be found in this co. The surface is mountainous, and the soil chiefly sandy loam. Pop. 3,924.—Also a county near the centre of the state of Ohio, containing an area, generally level, of 415 sq. m. It is drained by Deer and Paint creeks, and is tolerably fertile. Pop. 10,984. Its capital is Washington.—Also a county near the centre of the state of Georgia, comprising an area, generally level, of 545 sq. m., drained by the head-streams of Flint river, and possessing considerable fertility. Pop. 6,191. Its capital is Fayetteville.—Also a county in the NW of the state of | Alabama, containing a superficies of 1,250 sq. m., drained by Sipsey and Battahatchy rivers, and Luxapatilla creek, affluents of Tombigbee river. It is hilly, and generally fertile. Pop. 6,942. Its capital, which bears the same name, is 40 m. NW of Tuscaloosa, and 2 m. W of Sipsey river. - Also a county in the SW part of the state of Tennessee, comprising a surface of 576 sq. m., drained by Loosahatchy and Wolf rivers, and possessing great fertility. A railway extends from La Grange to Memphis, on the Mississippi, and a branch extends thence to Somerville, the capital of the co. Pop. 21,501.—Also a county near the centre of the state of Kentucky, containing a superficies of 275 sq. m., partly bordered on the S by Kentucky river, and drained by the sources of Elkhorn river. It forms an elevated table-land, and is extremely fertile. Pop. 22,194. Its capital is Lexington.—Also a county in the E part of the state of Indiana, containing an area of 400 sq. m., drained by the branches of Whitewater river, which unite in the centre of the co., and produce excellent water-power. It is in some parts tolerably fertile. Pop. 9,837. Its capital is Connersville.—Also a co. near the centre of the state of Illinois, comprising an area of 648 sq. m., drained by Kaskasia river and its branches. It is generally level, and has some prairie and woodland. Pop. 6,328. Its capital is Vandalia.—Also a township of Kennebec co., in the state of Maine, 18 m. WNW of Augusta, watered by a branch of Sandy river. Pop. 1,016.—Also a township of Seneca co., in the state of New York, 178 m. W of Albany, bounded by Cayuga lake on the E, on the W by Seneca lake, and on the NW by the river of that name, and watered by Canoga creek and some other small streams. Gypsum and limestone are found in this township. Pop. 3,731.—Also a village and port-of-entry in Hanover township, Chautauque co., in the same state, at the entrance of Silver creek into Lake Erie. —Also a village of Guilford township, Chenango co., in the same state.—Also a township of Alleghany co., in the state of Pennsylvania, 214 m. W of Harrisburg, drained by branches of Chartiers creek. Its surface is hilly, and its soil loamy and fertile. Coal is abundant. Pop. 2,484.—Also a village of Jefferson co., in the state of Mississippi, 80 m. SW of Jackson.—Also a township of Lawrence co., in the state of Ohio. Pop. 842.—Also a township of Hillsdale co., in the state of Michigan. Pop. 807 .- Also a village of Howard co., in the state of Missouri, 61 m. NW of Jefferson city, on the E side of Bonne Femme creek. Pop. 1,000.

FAYETTEVILLE, a village of Newfane township, Windham co., in the state of Vermont, 115 m. S of Montpelier.—Also a village of Maulins township, Onondaga co., in the state of New York, 123 m. NW of Albany, near the Erie canal. Pop. 800. -Also a village of Green township, Franklin co., in the state of Pennsylvania, 52 m. WSW of Harrisburg.—Also a village of Fayette co., in the state of Virginia, 289 m. W of Richmond, 2 m. W of New river, and near its junction with Gauley river.—Also a village, capital of Cumberland co., in the state of North Carolina, 1 m. from the W bank of Cape Fear river, 60 m. S of Raleigh. Pop. in 1820, 3,532; in 1840, 4,285. It is regularly built, and its streets are 100 ft. in width.—Also a village in Fayette co., in the state of Georgia, 85 m. WNW of Milledgeville. Pop. 300.—Also a village in Lincoln co., in the state of Tennessee, 86 m. S of Nashville, on the N side of Elk river.—Also a village of Washington co., in the state of Arkansas, 196 m. NW of Little Rock, on the NW side of White river. Pop. 425.—Also a vil- FAY of Springfield, on the W side of Kaskasia river. It was formerly called Pulliam's Ferry.

FAYEZULLAHGUNI, a district of India, in the prov. of Bhagulpur, on the r. bank of the Ganges. Area 256 sq. m. Pop. 89,000, chiefly Hindus. Its N extremity is low land, flooded by the river. The only towns are Kahalgang, which contains about 400 houses, and Gajarajguni.

FAYL-BILLOT (LE), a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Haute-Marne, arrond. of Langres. The cant, comprising 23 com., had a pop. of 13,022 in 1841; the town, 12 SE of Langres, had a pop. of 2,502.
FAYNOL, a township in the p. of Llanfair-Dyffrin-Clwyd, in Denbighshire. Pop. 194.

FAYUM, or FYOOM [Wilkinson], a prov. of Middle Egypt, stretching out into the desert, by which it is almost entirely surrounded. It consists of a valley or low table-land, nearly environed by a range of hills, of which those on the N and W sides, extending W to the extreme verge of the horizon, present a rugged and precipitous aspect, while the opposite range rises by a gentle ascent, in a series of undulating sand-hills. The Birket-el-Kerun, a lake about 35 m. long, and 7 m. broad, has generally been identified with the ancient lake Maris; but Professor Lepsius, and M. Linant, have recently discovered that the Birket-el-Kerun is the only lake in this prov., though it has neither been the work of human hands, nor did it ever water the ancient Crocodilopolis. A dry lake discovered by Linant, equal in extent and depth to the Birket-el-Kerun, and bounded by dams 160 ft. in breadth, is identified by him with the ancient Mæris; but this seems doubtful. Professor Lepsius' researches in this district have identified the pyramid of Mœris, who reigned from 2194 to 2151 B.C.; and the celebrated Labyrinth. Several roads lead from the Nile into the prov. of F. across the low Libyan chain of hills. The most frequented is that to Tomieh from El-Kafr. The Bahr-Yusuf enters F. at a point 12 m. NW of Benisuef, between the v. of Illahun on the NE, and the town of Hawara-el-Kebir on the SW. Beyond the Birket-el- Kerun stretch verdant plains, covered in many parts by groves of olive-trees, and intersected by canals which have the appearance of rivers, possessing wooded banks, and flowing in meandering courses, and with a perceptible current in many places. Generally speaking, every v. has its own stream. This territory formed the ancient Arsinoë, described by Strabo as possessed of the highest fertility, and cultivated like a garden. The waters of the Nile were then received by several openings into this enclosed valley, and overflowed it as they did the Delta. But F. now suffers severely from want of watering; and the sands of the desert, wafted by the winds, have made many inroads, and rendered some spots of it entirely unproductive. These physical evils are aggravated by the predatory inroads of the Arabs, who roam through the western deserts. Two of their tribes have, however, established themselves in the territory of F., and appear to be gradually adopting a pastoral life, and pay a ground rent to the pasha of about 9 piastres, or 2s. per feddan, which is somewhat less than an acre. [Bowring.] The whole extent of the cultivable part of F. measures about 23 m. from N to S, and 28 m. from E to W. [Wilkinson.] The grain principally raised is durra, which requires less moisture than any other; rye and barley are grown only on the best-watered lands; flax is also an important product. Cotton and the sugar-cane are raised in limited quantity; but the culture of indigo is considerable. Date-trees are planted in great numbers in the W part of the territory. One of the lage of St. Clair co., in the state of Illinois, 100 m. S | most extensive articles of cultivation is that of rosebushes. These are disposed at a distance of <math>2\frac{1}{2}</math> ft. from each other, in little squares, between rivulets, for the purpose of being thoroughly moistened. They are planted in winter, and for the first fortnight must be daily watered. The cultivation is expensive and laborious, but might be profitable, as the demand is great for the extensive manufactories of rose-water and attar carried on in the capital. Mr. St. John informs us that the rose-plantations of one European speculator occupy 30,000 acres in the F.; but it is seldom that any native cultivator has more than a single feddan dedicated to the production of rose-trees. A feddan gives from 6 to 7 quintals of roses. Much attention is also paid to the rearing of cattle. The sheep after shearing are protected from the heat of the sun by a covering of palm-leaves. The horses are smaller than those of Upper Egypt. The only camels used are those bought from the Arabs of the desert.—The prov. of F. is distinguished above most other parts of Egypt by its manufacturing industry. Cloths are made here of woollen, linen, and cotton. All the flax raised in the country is manufactured here in pieces of between 50 and 60 ft. long, and bearing various prices according to their fineness. The yarn is bleached before it is woven, by being put in a ley of natron and chalk, and afterwards washed in cold water. From the tow a species of sackcloth is made, which forms a great article of export, as from 20,000 to 30,000 pieces are yearly conveyed to Cairo. The country does not furnish cotton for the supply of its own fabrics; the greater portion is therefore brought from Cairo and Lower Egypt. At the public markets of F., which are held on fixed days, the Arabs supply themselves with linens and other small wares used in their wandering mode of life; and give in exchange dates and other hard fruits, and cattle. The communication with Cairo is carried on by caravans, which set out weekly from the village of Tamieh. They carry shawls, sackcloth, mats, rose-water, figs, olives, dates, &c., and bring back raw cotton, coffee, soap, cloths, and several other European commodities. - This prov. is governed by a cashef under the bey of Benisuef. FAYUM, MEDINET-EL-FAYUM, OF MEDEE'NEH [Wilkinson], the cap. of the above prov., situated between 12 and 13 m. S of the Birket-el-Kerun, in N lat. 29° 29′, E long. 31° 1′ 30″, on the site of the ancient Crocodilopolis. It is divided into two parts by a canal from the Bahr-Yusuf. Its pop. in 1824 was estimated at 5,000. It forms the central point on 1,019. which all the canals by which the country is watered unite. It is also the principal seat of the manufactures of the prov. It is about 1 m. in circumf.; the houses, consisting merely of bricks dried in the sun, compose an assemblage of gloomy huts.—The remains of the celebrated Labyrinth lie between the town of Hawara-el-Sogair and F.
FAZELEY, a township in the p. of Tamworth, co. of Stafford, 2 m. S of Tamworth. Pop. 1,490.
FAZILPUR, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Bhalpur, in N lat. 28° 30'.

FE' (Santa), a city of New Mexico, in N lat. 35° 44'06"; and 7 hours 04 min. W of Fort Leavenworth; 50 m. direct distance S by W of Taos; at an alt. of 6,846 ft. above sea-level. [Emory.] It is situated on a small creek which comes down from the mountains, and 5 m. below the town disappears in the granitic sands; but is represented in Abert's map as pursuing a SSW course to the l. bank of the Riodel-Norte, and falling into that river 30 m. below Santa Fé. The town is about 1 m. in length, and mainly consists of three streets. The houses are of mud bricks, in the Spanish style, generally of one story. The apartments are arranged in the form of a square, and open into an interior quadrangle. It has two | tle. Pop. in 1801, 197; in 1841, 265.—Also a town- AND THE PERSON OF THE PROPERTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PA FEA churches, the magnificence of whose spires forms a striking contrast to the appearance of the other buildings. The public square is in the centre of the town; and on the N side of it is situated the government-house. Pike supposed the pop. to be 5,000; Humboldt estimated it at 3,600; Emory, in 1846, at from 2,000 to 4,000.—The road from Fort Leavenworth to this place presents few obstacles for a railway, and will probably become one of the great mercantile routes between the United States and the provs. of Sonora, Durango, and Southern California. The port of Galveston in Texas is 500 m. distant from Santa Fé; Philadelphia, 4,000 m.

FE (SANTA), a province of the Argentine republic, stretching between the r. bank of the Parana on the E, and the prov. of Cordova on the W. It was in former times one of the most important sections of the vice-royalty. Its estancieros were amongst the richest in the whole country: and their cattle-farms covered large tracts on the E shore of the river in the Entre Rios. Its pop. is now reduced to from 15,000 to 20,000.—Its cap., of the same name, is situated on the r. bank of the Parana, at the confluence of the Salado. Pop. 6,000. Its situation is highly favourable to the cultivation of a transit trade between Buenos-Ayres and the provs. N of Cordova.
FE (SANTA). See BOGOTA, and ANTIOQUIA.
FE (SANTA), a town of Spain, in the prov. of Grenada, on the Genil. Pop. 2,000.
FEACHORY, a small river of Scotland, in Perthshire, which falls into the Garry at Stravan, after an E course of 13 m.

FEACLE, a parish in co. Clare, 5 m. W by N of Scarriff. Area 36,972 acres. Pop. in 1831, 8,744; in 1841, 10,156. The surface of this large p. consists of the loftiest and wildest W uplands of the co. Near its centre, at an alt. of 159 ft. above sea-level, lies Lough Graney. Area 972 acres.

FEALE, a river of Ireland, which rises near Coolnakenny, in the co. of Limerick, and uniting with the river Gall, in co. Kerry, takes the name of Cashen; after which it falls into the mouth of the Shannon, 11 m. above Kerry-head. It is navigable over 6 m. from its mouth.
FEAR (CAPE), a promontory on the coast of N. Carolina, the S point of Smith's island, in N lat. 33° 48′, W long. 78° 9′ [Raper].
FEARING, a township in Washington co., in the state of Ohio, U. S., 109 m. ESE of Columbus. Pop.

FEARN, a village and parish of Ross-shire, bounded on the S by the Moray frith. Pop. in 1801, 1,528; in 1841, 1,914. The v. is 4 m. SE of Tain. -Also a parish of Forfarshire, near the centre of the co., 7 m. WNW of Brechin. Pop. in 1801, 408; in 1841, 404.

FEATHER RIVER, or RIO-DE-LAS-PLUMAS, a river of California, the largest tributary of the Sacramento, which rises in the N part of the Californian range, and flows in a course of 100 m. nearly due S to the Sacramento, which it joins on the l. bank, 15 m. above New Helvetia, and 90 m. from the bay of San Francisco. The towns of Fremont and Vernon have been founded on opposite sides of the Sacramento, at the confluence of F. river; and Nicolaus and Eliza on the r. bank of the F. itself at some distance up the river. Steamers ascend this river, the gold-mining region upon which commences below the outlying spurs of the Californian range, 30 m. from Marysville, at the mouth of the Yuba, and extend back upon the mountains to a distance of about 80 m.

FEATHERSTONE, a township in the p. of Haltwhistle, in Northumberland, 3\frac{3}{4} m. SW of Haltwhisship in the p. of Wolverhampton, in Stafford, 6 m. S of Wolverhampton.—Also a parish in the W. R. of Yorkshire, 3 m. W by N of Pontefract. Area 4,050 acres. Pop. in 1801, 800; in 1841, 1,005.

FECAMP, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Seine-Inferieure, arrond. of Le Havre.—The cant., comprising 11 com., had a pop. of 17,947 in 1841.—The town, which is 21 m. NNE of Havre, had a pop. of 9,418 in 1841, part of whom are employed in fishing, part in the manufacture of woollens, lace, and linen. Its port on the English channel, at the mouth of a river of the same name, is a good one.
FECHAIN, a commune of France, in the dep. of Nord, cant. of Arleux. Pop. 1,110.

FECHO-DOS-MORROS, a contracted portion of the river Paraguay in S. America, in S lat. 21° 20', forming the limit between High and Low Paraguay. It is formed by a chain of low mountains, through which the river here finds its way.
FECKENHAM, a parish in Worcestershire, 7 m. ESE of Droitwich. Area 6,740 acres. Pop. 2,800.

FEDA'LAH, a small port on the W coast of Marocco, on a promontory which has sometimes been mistaken for an island, and bears E 3 N 13 m. from Cape Dar-el-Beïda. The small bay in front of the v. affords anchorage in 5 or 6 fath. close to shore. A fine and fertile country stretches inwards from the coast here, and a considerable exportation of corn formerly took place from this harbour. To the S of the town, the plains are intersected by extensive lakes and marshes bordered with thickets of high reeds, and abounding with water-fowl.
FEDERALSBURG, a village in Dorchester co., in the state of Maryland, U.S., on the E side of Chesapeake-bay, 20 m. NE of Cambridge.
FEDERALTON, a village in Athens co., in the state of Ohio, U. S., 92 m. SE of Columbus, at the mouth of Federal creek.
FEDER-SEE, a lake of Wurtemberg, a little to the E of Buchau, about 12 m. in circumf. The Kanzach issues from this lake, and flows into the Danube.
FEDJERAH (MERSA), a small bay on the coast of Yemen, 16 m. N & E from Mocha,

FEDMIN, a town of Central Egypt, in the prov. of Fayum, between the Birket-el-Kerun and the town of Medinet-el-Fayum. It stands on a high mound of earth and rubbish, on a branch of the Bahr-Yussuf, and is chiefly inhabited by Christian Copts.
FEEJEE. See Fiji.
FEELSTED, a parish in Essex, 10 m. N of Chelmsford. Area 7,170 acres. Pop. 1,798.
FEERING, a parish in Essex, 2 m. E of Coggeshall. Area 2,430 acres. Pop. 817.
FEESURA, a town of Kaarta, in W. Africa, 28 m. W of Kemmu.
FEFINANES, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 24 m. N of Vigo, on Arosa bay.
FEGARI, or FAGARI, a town of Japan, in the island of Nifon, 70 m. SSE of Meaco.
FE'GE'AC, a commune of France, in the dep. of Loire-Inferieure, cant. of Saint-Nicolas. Pop. 2,272.

FEGERSHEIM, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Bas-Rhin, 7 m. S of Strasburg, on the railway to Colmar. Pop. 1,771. FEHRABAD, FAHRABAD, or FERAHABAD, a village of Persia, in the prov. of Mazanderan, near the S coast of the Caspian sea, at the mouth of the Tejin, 126 m. W of Astrabad. It carries on some trade in rice, salt, fish, naphtha, nordoneh or pomegranate seeds, and pottery. Formerly the pop. was computed at 16,000 persons, principally the descendants FEL of some 70 or 80 miserable huts; and its trade is very insignificant. The environs produce sugar, cotton, and silk. A large sturgeon-fishery is carried on in the river at this place.
FEHRBELLIN, a town of Prussia, in Brandenburg, circle of Ost-Havelland, 28 m. NW of Berlin. Pop. 1,509.
FEICHTING, a village of Austria, in Carniola, in the circle of Laybach.
FEIDENHEIM, a large village of Germany, in the grand duchy of Baden, with 1,200 inhabitants. 3 m. E of Manheim.
FEIGNIES, a village of France, in the dep. of Nord, cant. of Bavay, 3 m. W of Maubeuge. Pop. 1,846.
FEILLENS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Ain, cant. of Bage-le-Chatel. Pop. 2,526.
FEIRA, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Beira, 13 m. SSE of Oporto. Pop. 2,000.

FEIRAN, or FARAN (WADI), an extensive valley in the peninsula of Sinai, extending from the E coast of the gulf of Suez, about the parallel of 29° N, in a SSE direction to Mount Sinai. For the first couple of hours from its W extremity, it runs SE by E in almost a straight line, and is tolerably level, with a breadth of from 400 to 600 yds., between ridges of sandstone rocks. From the mouth of Wadi Rumman, which runs into it from the NNE, it assumes a winding-course, but its plants and bushes increase; and at the mouth of Wadi Aleikat, about 6 hours from its mouth, date-trees, and plots of nebk—Rhamnus lotus—begin to abound, and extend upwards for 4 m. Tobacco, cucumbers, gourds, melons, and onions, are likewise grown in this district. Burckhardt describes the ruins of an ancient city upon the mountains on both sides, where the Wadi F. is joined by the Wadi Aleikat. The houses are small, and built entirely of stone, with flat roofs. Rüppell found here the remains of a church which he assigns to the 5th cent. The Wadi F. is connected with the Wadiesh-Sheikh, which issuing from the high central granite cliffs of the peninsula, at first in an E direction, sweeps round N and W, and joins the F.- Wilson. -Robinson.-Niebuhr.
FEIRREIRA-D'AVES, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Beira, 20 m. NE of Viseu. Pop. 1,600.

FEISTRITZ, or WISTERZA, a town of Austria, in Styria, in the circle and 20 m. NE of Cilly. Pop. 650.—Also a town of Carniola, circle of Laybach.— Also a town of Styria, in the circle and 9 m. NNW of Grätz, on the r. bank of the Mur. Lead is extensively mined here.
FEISUN, a town of Asiatic Turkey, in the pash. of Diarbekir, 40 m. N of Diarbekir.
FELAHIYAH. See DORAK.
FELANICHE, a considerable town on the E coast of the island of Majorca, 27 m. ESE of Palma. Pop. about 8,000.
FELBACH, a large village of Wurtemberg, in the Neckar circle. Pop. 2,837.
FELBRIGG, a parish in Norfolk, 2 m. SW of Cromer. Area 1,440 acres. Pop. 133.
FELDA, a village of Germany, in the grand duchy of Hesse-Darmstadt, bail. of Alsfeld. Pop. 880.
FELDBERG, a village of Baden, 3 m. SSE of Mullheim.—Also a mountain in Baden. See Baden. -Also a small river of Bavaria, which falls into the Werra 2 m. NE of Vacha.

FELDKIRCH, a town of Austrian Tyrol, in the Vorarlberg, on the river Ill, 20 m. SSW of Bregenz. It has manufactories of cotton cloth, and of kirschwasser. Pop. 1,590. In the neighbourhood is the Adlerberg, across which a fine road has been made at the expense of government. F. was the of Armenians and Georgians. It is now a collection | scene of some severe fighting in the campaign of 1799, between the French and Austrians.—Also a village of Baden, near the Rhine, 10 m, W by S of Freyburg.
FELDKIRCHEN, a town of Austria, in Carinthia, 13 m. NW of Klagenfurt, near the Glan river.
FELDRENNACH, a town of Wurtemberg, in the circle of the Black forest. Pop. 852.

FELDSBERG, a town of Lower Carinthia, 4 m. NE of Saxenburg, 45 m. NW of Klagenfurt.—Also a town of Lower Austria, near the confines of Moravia, 38 m. NNE of Vienna. Pop. 2,474. Prince Lichtenstein has a magnificent chateau here.
FELDSTETTEN, a village of Wurtemberg, 11 m. E by S of Urach.
FELE (SAN), a town of Naples, in the prov. of Basilicata, 9 m. SSW of Melfi.
FELEGYHAZA, a town of Hungary, in Little Cumania, 66 m. SE of Buda. Pop. in 1845, 17,000. It has a considerable trade in corn, fruit, cattle, and wine.
FELENGA, a small island in the gulf of Venice, near the coast of Istria, in N lat. 45° 52'.

FELICE (SAN), a town of Spain, in the prov. of Leon, 12 m. NNW of Ciudad-Rodrigo.—Also a town of Italy, in the Papal states, 10 m. WSW of Teracina. Pop. 500.—Also a small town of the duchy of Modena, 17 m. NNE of Modena.—Also a village of Lombardy, 16 m. ENE of Brescia, on the Lagodi-Garda.
FELICES-DE-LOS-GALLIGOS, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 22 m. WSW of Salamanca, near the Agueda.
FELICIEN (SAINT), a canton and town of France, in the dep. of Ardeche. Pop. of cant. in 1841, 10,805; of town, 9 m. W of Tournon, 2,200.

FELICUDI, one of the Lipari islands, 10 m. W of Salina, the ancient Phanicusa. It consists chiefly of volcanic rock, and has three high mountains upon it. It is 9 m. in circumf., and has some good cornland, and produces oil, wine, flax and fruit. The pop. is about 800.

FELIO-SECERRA (SAN), a town of Spain, in the prov. and 38 m. N of Barcelona, near the r. bank of the Gavarresa. FELIPE (SAN), OF PHILIP DE XATIVA, a considerable town of Spain, in the prov. of Valencia, situated on the declivity of a calcareous mountain, 29 m. SSW of Valencia. Pop. 15,000. It has a castle, and some remains of Roman and Moorish antiquities. The Roman name of this place was Setabis, which was changed by the Moors to Xativa. In 1706, in the war-of-succession, it was taken by assault and burned; and King Philip, on ordering it to be rebuilt, gave it the name of San-Felipe. The adjacent country is productive, particularly in rice.

FELIPPE (SAN), a town of Venezuela, 150 m. WSW of Caraccas, which from a miserable village has grown up to be a flourishing town, the seat of industry and of commerce. It is regularly built; the streets are on a line, and broad, and the parish church is handsome. The country around is extremely fertile, and the inhabitants, who amount to 7,000, cultivate cocoa, indigo, coffee, and cotton.— There are numerous inconsiderable settlements of Indians of this name in South America.—Also a town of Mexico, in the prov. and 20 m. N of Guanaxuato. Pop. 2,000.—Also a village of New Mexico, on the r. bank of the Rio-del-Norte, in N lat. 35° 25′, 40 m. SSW of Santa Fe, and at an alt. of 5,158 ft. above sea-level. The valley of the river is here very narrow. Its W banks are steep and crowned by seams of basalt.—Also a town of Chili. on the river Aconcagua, the capital of the prov. of Aconcagua. Pop. 5,000.—Also a village of Brazil, in the prov. of Bahia, comarca of Rio-da-Contas. FEL
FELIS-DA-COSTA, a large lake of Brazil, in the prov. of Goyaz, on the Serra-dos-Pirenéos. It is 40 m. in circuit, and discharges its waters into the Maranhão.

FELIS (SAO), a town of Brazil, in the prov. and 280 m. NNE of Goyaz, and comarca of Cavalcante, on a small river of the same name, an affluent of the Tocantins, which contains gold in quantities. It is crossed by the great road from Goyaz to Para .- Also a parish in the prov. of Maranhão, at the confluence of the Balças with the Parnahiba. Cotton is extensively cultivated in this locality for the markets of Caxias and Itapicurú.—Also a small town on the river Paraguaçu, in the parish of Bahia, opposite to Cachoeira.
